div.p: William by the grace of God king of the Scots to the bishops, abbots, earls, barons, justices, sheriffs, provosts, officials, and all worthy men of all his land clerk and lay sends greeting. Let present and future know us to have granted and by this our charter to have made firm to God and St Mary and the church of Melrose and the monks serving God in that place, in free and quit and perpetual alms, the church of Hassendean with lands and teinds and all that pertains to the church itself, assigned by Jocelin bishop of Glasgow by our advice for the reception and support of the poor and of pilgrims coming to the church of Melrose. Since there was a dispute about this church between us and the same bishop, to which our donation of this church pertains, we have granted our right which we had or could have had in it to the same bishop to be disposed to that church according to God. And the bishop himself, so that church’s harvest in the works of charity should be distributed and perpetually be converted to good uses has by pious regard conveyed and affirmed that same church with all that belongs to it, for the sake of the souls of all our predecessors and his own, both for the salvation and also the souls of us and him, and of all our heirs and his successors, by our advice and consent, for the perpetual use of the poor and of pilgrims, to the church and convent of Melrose. In order that this pious action, both reasonable and complete, should endure for ever we have granted and by this our charter affirmed the aforesaid church of Hassendean with all its pertinents to God and St Mary and the church of Melrose in free and everlasting alms for the afore-named holy uses; to be held for ever as freely, quitely, fully and honourably as any parish church in our kingdom is as freely, quitely, fully, and honourably held and possessed by any conventual church; saving a pension of twenty shillings to be paid each year by the monks to the church of Glasgow in respect of the church of Hassendean, just as the charter of Jocelin bishop of Glasgow bears witness; and lest there could be any dispute in the future between us or our heirs or those who shall have held of the aforesaid manor and those monks over the pasture of the aforesaid toun, it is provided by our command and by the consideration of our worthy men, we have granted and by this our charter affirmed that the aforesaid monks ought to have for ever in the pasture of the aforesaid toun, to wit, two hundred ewes, sixteen oxen, and four cows, firmly forbidding that anyone commit any injury or vexation to them over this, on pain of our full forfeiture. As witnesses: Jocelin, bishop of Glasgow; Richard, bishop of Moray; Archibald, abbot of Dunfermline; Earl David our brother; Hugh our chancellor; William of Lindsay, justice; Philip de Valognes; David of Lindsay; Hugh de Normanville; Walter of Berkeley our chamberlain; Herbert Chalmers. At Melrose.
